&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a#giveaway )

The 340B drug pricing program is supposed to help vulnerable patients acces=
s medicines at qualifying hospitals and clinics. It=E2=80=99s meant to be a=
 safety net for those who really need it. So why is the 340B program paddin=
g profits for large hospitals, PBMs and chain pharmacies?

Hospitals that participate in the 340B program contract with more than 33,0=
00 pharmacies to dispense the program=E2=80=99s drug prescriptions. More th=
an 40% of these pharmacies have financial ties to one of the three largest =
PBMs =E2=80=93 CVS Health, Express Scripts and OptumRx. 340B hospitals and =
the PBM-owned pharmacies they contract with are profiting off discounted me=
dicines while uninsured patients are left paying full price for their medic=
ines. Let=E2=80=99s fix 340B so it better helps patients. ( https://phrma.o=
rg/policy-issues/340b?utm_campaign=3D2024-q3-340b-340-340BPBMPuckTBTBhighim=
pact-bargraph&utm_medium=3Dpai_spn&utm_source=3Dpck&utm_content=3Dall-pol-b=
nr_sta-340BPBMBarGraph&utm_campaign=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Brightest+-+SUBSCRIB=
ERS+%287%2F22%2F24%29&utm_content=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Brightest+-+SUBSCRIBER=
S+%287%2F21%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_=
term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a#giveaway )

Redefining Kamala

Harris has many challenges ahead. The first is that despite the weekend=E2=
=80=99s hype, Biden=E2=80=99s vice president is still unpopular. Her averag=
e approval rating is only around 38 percent, no better than Biden=E2=80=99s=
, though her disapproval ratings are lower, suggesting some room to grow. I=
n horse race polls since the debate disaster in Atlanta, Harris has been ru=
nning slightly behind Trump, about where Biden was. Democrats right now are=
 hyped=E2=80=94but they are still losing. Harris has to improve her poll nu=
mbers and win over the couch-sitters who were resisting Biden. Specifically=
, she has to prove that she can rally the younger voters and voters of colo=
r who have been stiff-arming Biden throughout the campaign, in particular y=
oung Black and Hispanic men. She also has to give people considering a thir=
d-party candidate a reason not to. That=E2=80=99s a tall order.

What=E2=80=99s going to be critical for both campaigns is defining Harris=
=E2=80=94literally, in the next few days and weeks. Despite the onrush of e=
nthusiasm from Democratic die-hards, Harris, like any V.P., has been an aft=
erthought to most Americans for the last three years. I spent some time ( h=
ttps://puck.news/the-kamala-comeback/?utm_campaign=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Brigh=
test+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%287%2F22%2F24%29&utm_content=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Brighte=
st+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%287%2F21%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dc=
ustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) with her recently, and as I=E2=
=80=99ve written, I got the sense she has improved as a politician since he=
r lackluster 2020 Democratic primary campaign. But the klieg lights of a ge=
neral election are an entirely different beast. On Monday morning, I texted=
 a Trump advisor asking how they plan to run against Kamala. The advisor ca=
lled her =E2=80=9Cdangerously liberal=E2=80=9D and said Harris would be sad=
dled with Biden=E2=80=99s record on border security and
inflation. =E2=80=9CHarris inflation,=E2=80=9D this
person said.

The Trump campaign will go scorched earth against Harris on immigration and=
 prices, and will try to paint her as an out-of-touch California leftist wi=
th a penchant for goofy and unserious statements. They=E2=80=99ll micro-tar=
get Black voters with =E2=80=9CKamala Is A Cop=E2=80=9D messaging about her=
 record as San Francisco District Attorney, and they=E2=80=99ll blame Harri=
s for the war in Gaza in targeted messaging to young progressives and voter=
s in Michigan. And that=E2=80=99s just what to expect from the campaign. Wh=
o knows what the MAGA internet=E2=80=94and Trump himself=E2=80=94will say a=
bout a Black and South Asian woman running for president.

For the Harris campaign, it=E2=80=99s imperative that they tap their gargan=
tuan fundraising haul immediately to bombard swing states with paid adverti=
sing reintroducing Harris to the country on their terms. I=E2=80=99m curiou=
s what messaging they settle on. The ads will almost certainly focus on pro=
tecting abortion rights, a topic better suited
to Harris than Biden and the centerpiece of her campaign efforts for the la=
st year. The campaign will also try to graft Biden=E2=80=99s more popular a=
ccomplishments=E2=80=94lowering the cost of prescription drugs, creating mo=
re than 15 million jobs, passing gun reform legislation, historic investmen=
ts in clean energy=E2=80=94onto Harris. Maybe we=E2=80=99ll get some prosec=
utor-versus-felon messaging that the MSNBC crowd so desperately craves. But=
 pay attention to that very first Harris campaign ad, whenever it drops, be=
cause it will tell us a lot about where this campaign is going.

The Independent Factor

So what do voters think about Harris as a person and a candidate? What stre=
ngths and weaknesses does she bring to the campaign? As part of our 2024 pa=
rtnership with the polling firm Echelon Insights, we asked voters ( https:/=
/puck.news/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/July-2024-Voter-Omnibus-Topline-PUCKv=
2.pdf?utm_campaign=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Brightest+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%287%2F22%2F2=
4%29&utm_content=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Brightest+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%287%2F21%2F24%=
29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da=
42abd12a ) for open-ended responses to the simple question, =E2=80=9CHow wo=
uld you describe Harris in a few words?=E2=80=9D Our poll was in the field =
over the weekend, from Friday to Sunday, before the big Biden news and the =
arrival of Harris as the likely nominee. But the qualitative data is still =
valuable because it offers a glimpse into how
the electorate sees Harris as she embarks on this unprecedented four-month =
sprint of a campaign.

About a third of likely voters ( https://puck.news/wp-content/uploads/2024/=
07/July-Omnibus-Crosstabs-PUCK-with-OE.pdf?utm_campaign=3DThe+Best+%26+The+=
Brightest+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%287%2F22%2F24%29&utm_content=3DThe+Best+%26+The+Br=
ightest+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%287%2F21%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_sourc=
e=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) (29 percent) offered positi=
ve answers describing her as =E2=80=9Ccompetent, capable, strong=E2=80=9D o=
r something similar. More than half of Democrats responded in positive term=
s, and Black voters and young voters were more likely to say nice things ab=
out Harris than other subgroups, which is good news for Democrats who have =
been pining after those voters. But crucially, only 21 percent of independe=
nts responded positively when asked about Harris. Independents were just as=
 likely to call Harris some variation of =E2=80=9Cunlikeable=E2=80=9D or
=E2=80=9Cunprepared=E2=80=9D (21 percent) or respond with negative name-cal=
ling (16 percent). The most common response from independent voters on Harr=
is? That they didn=E2=80=99t know much about her at all, or had a neutral o=
pinion (21 percent).

=E2=80=9CDemocrats think of Kamala Harris as competent, capable, and strong=
,=E2=80=9D said Kristen Soltis Anderson, a co-founder of Echelon. =E2=80=9C=
Republicans, on the other hand, don=E2=80=99t hesitate to say they think sh=
e is unlikeable or unprepared to lead on her own. Independents, as you migh=
t expect, are often somewhere in the middle or simply say they don=E2=80=99=
t feel like they know enough quite yet.=E2=80=9D This is, obviously, why th=
e race to define Harris in the next few weeks is so critical for both campa=
igns. Democratic and Republican voters view Harris through their expected p=
artisan lenses. And Trump, of course, is fully defined across the spectrum.=
 But our poll suggests that opinions of Harris are still being formed among=
 independent voters. Their
eventual views might sway the entire election.

We also found in our poll that Biden made the right decision to leave the r=
ace, at least according to Democrats. The Echelon poll asked Democrats, =E2=
=80=9CWhich do you think would be the best decision for Biden=E2=80=99s leg=
acy, even if neither option is perfect?=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94to run for a secon=
d term, or =E2=80=9Cleaving the race to make room for the next generation o=
f Democratic leaders.=E2=80=9D About half of Democrats (49 percent) said it=
 would be better for Biden=E2=80=99s legacy to leave the race. And again, t=
hat was before he made his actual announcement. Only 40 percent of Democrat=
s said it would be better for Biden=E2=80=99s legacy to run for a second te=
rm.

As for the rush by Democratic elites to anoint Harris as the nominee, most =
Democratic voters were supportive, but not exactly a dominant majority. Ask=
ed if they wanted Harris or someone else if Biden were to leave the race, 6=
2 percent of Democratic voters said Harris, while 18 percent said
someone else, and 20 percent were unsure.

Democratic voters also seem more open than party insiders to the idea of a =
competitive nomination before or at the Democratic convention, whatever sha=
pe that would take. As of Sunday, 48 percent of Democrats said they want a =
quick vote for Harris to become the nominee, versus 38 percent who said the=
y favored =E2=80=9Can open contest at the convention with numerous candidat=
es.=E2=80=9D But with Democrats in Washington rallying around Harris as the=
ir horse for November, it seems clear the latter group will not get their w=
ish.

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">In stark contrast t=

uld still challenge her at the now open convention next month. But in reali=
ty, the Harris nomination was essentially sewn up within a few hours of Bid=
en=E2=80=99s announcement. A critical mass of congresspeople, senators, int=
erest groups, delegates, party chairs, and pundits quickly fell in line beh=
ind her, along with leaders and elders. =E2=80=9CWe got where we=E2=80=99re=
 meant to be, and landing the plane is everything,=E2=80=9D a Democratic co=
nsultant told me, audibly relieved.<br/><br/>Hill Republicans, for their pa=
rt, were clearly dismayed over how quickly the party coalesced around Harri=
s and the growing likelihood that she won=E2=80=99t
face competition in a raucous open primary. If anything, the lack of appeti=
te for an open contest speaks to how <i>unnerving</i> the last month was fo=
r professional political tacticians. Indeed, many Democrats rationalized <i=
>weeks</i> <i>ago </i>that Harris was the obvious, and perhaps easiest, rep=
lacement for Biden. The party=E2=80=99s current euphoria recalls the Republ=
icans=E2=80=99 collective exhale after they finally selected <b>Mike Johnso=
n</b> as speaker last fall. But while Republicans have made a blood sport o=
f tearing each other apart, this scale of infighting was novel for Democrat=
s, who=E2=80=99ve been remarkably unified in the Trump era. <br/><br/>The p=
ast 48 hours have also crystallized <b>Nancy Pelosi</b>=E2=80=99s position =
as the generational leader of the Democratic Party. It=E2=80=99s hard to fi=
nd a Democrat willing to argue that any other figure=E2=80=94not <b>Bill</b=
> <b>Clinton</b> or <b>Chuck Schumer</b> or even <b>Obama</b>=E2=80=94could=
 have organized the lobbying effort to defenestrate
Biden. Once Pelosi went on <i>Morning Joe </i>two weeks ago to encourage th=
e president to reconsider the decision that he=E2=80=99d repeatedly and ang=
rily insisted was already settled, it became clear that Biden=E2=80=99s day=
s as a candidate were numbered.<br/><br/>As my partner <b>John Heilemann </=
b><u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt=

5e79" style=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" class=
=3D"sdf">wrote</a></u> yesterday, =E2=80=9CBiden was well aware that Pelosi=
 was his main adversary in his battle to stay on the ticket, which is no su=
rprise.=E2=80=9D John=E2=80=99s sources added that =E2=80=9Cshe had spoken =
directly to him more than once in the past week to paint a picture of how t=

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">It=E2=80=99s an old=
 clich=C3=A9 in politics that a campaign=E2=80=99s best day is usually the =
first day. <b>Kamala Harris</b>, the Democratic Party=E2=80=99s new presump=
tive presidential nominee, has given that phrase extraordinary new meaning =
over the last 24 hours. <br/><br/>With precious few weeks until the Democra=
tic National Convention in Chicago, Harris essentially took over the entire=
 machinery of her party overnight, inheriting not just <b>Joe Biden</b>=E2=
=80=99s behemoth presidential campaign in Wilmington, but also the broad su=
pport from party leaders around the country who rushed to endorse her after=
 Biden=E2=80=99s historic statement exiting the race on Sunday. Would-be ch=
allengers for the nomination like <b>Josh Shapiro</b> and <b>Gretchen Whitm=
er </b>quickly fell in line. House Speaker Emerita <b>Nancy Pelosi </b>endo=
rsed on Monday, too, saying, =E2=80=9CI have full
confidence [Harris] will lead us to victory in November=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94an=
d well, she better, considering Pelosi=E2=80=99s ruthless maneuvering over =
the last few weeks to force Biden from the race.<br/><br/>Delegates to the =
D.N.C.=E2=80=94the roughly 4,000 party activists who will anoint Harris in =
Chicago=E2=80=94quickly demonstrated their eagerness to move on from the Bi=
den drama of the last few weeks. A rush of state party organizations declar=
ed their support for Harris after she announced her campaign on Sunday. <b>=
Michael Kapp</b>, a D.N.C. member from California, told me that he believes=
 Harris will likely have enough convention delegates lined up to secure the=
 nomination by Tuesday, if not by the time this email hits your inbox tonig=
ht. =E2=80=9CI have been involved in Democratic Party activism for two deca=
des, and I have never seen anything as unifying across the party as Kamala =
Harris running for president,=E2=80=9D Kapp told me. Any hurt feelings over=
 the Biden flame wars of the past few weeks? Evaporated.
=E2=80=9CEveryone is on board,=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=80=9CWe are all on boa=
rd.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>Harris=E2=80=99s fundraising numbers are absolutely s=
taggering. In just 24 hours, the campaign has raised $81 million. That=E2=
=80=99s the biggest single-day fundraising total in American history=E2=80=
=94a sum that erases the cash advantage <b>Trump</b> has built up in recent=
 weeks. Democratic donors=E2=80=94small, large, and mega alike=E2=80=94had =
been holding back on giving since the Atlanta debate, and down-ballot Senat=
e and House campaigns were suffering from the party-wide anguish and confus=
ion, too, I=E2=80=99m reliably told. The flood of Harris money has erased t=
hat problem, just in time for the newly minted Harris campaign to flex what=
 will surely be a bonkers July fundraising total come next month. I have ne=
ver seen anything like it.<br/><br/>The surge of Democratic excitement has =
<b>Donald Trump</b>=E2=80=99s campaign on its heels, at least for now. They=
 were counting on running against =E2=80=9Ca corpse,=E2=80=9D as one Trump =
official told me
recently. In recent weeks, polls showed Trump opening up comfortable leads =
in every battleground state, Mar-a-Lago viewed the G.O.P. convention as a (=
mostly) on-message success, and the assassination attempt on Trump earlier =
this month gave the campaign an almost divine sense of inevitability agains=
t a hapless Democratic incumbent. Instead, we now enter the final 100 or so=
 days with a completely new presidential race=E2=80=94one in which the Trum=
p campaign has to rejigger its messaging against a woman of color while gra=
ppling with a level of Democratic enthusiasm that Biden had been failing to=
 summon all year. <br/><br/>The press coverage, too, has been turned on its=
 head. Just days ago, the media was feasting on Democratic infighting and t=
he coming demise of the Biden campaign. At one point during the Biden death=
 watch this month, multiple Republican friends messaged me with wide-eyed a=

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Harris has many cha=

=E2=80=99s vice president is still unpopular. Her average approval rating i=

roval ratings are lower, suggesting some room to grow. In horse race polls =
since the debate disaster in Atlanta, Harris has been running slightly behi=
nd Trump, about where Biden was. Democrats right now are hyped=E2=80=94but =
they are still losing. Harris has to improve her poll numbers and win over =
the couch-sitters who were resisting Biden. Specifically, she has to prove =
that she can rally the younger voters and voters of color who have been sti=
ff-arming Biden throughout the campaign, in particular young Black and Hisp=
anic men. She also has to give people considering a third-party candidate a=
 reason not to. That=E2=80=99s a tall order.
<br/><br/>What=E2=80=99s going to be critical for both campaigns is <i>defi=
ning</i> Harris=E2=80=94literally, in the next few days and weeks. Despite =
the onrush of enthusiasm from Democratic die-hards, Harris, like any V.P., =
has been an afterthought to most Americans for the last three years. I <u><=
a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2Uktxc=

ap their gargantuan fundraising haul <i>immediately</i> to bombard swing st=
ates with paid advertising reintroducing Harris to the country on their ter=

e middle or simply say they don=E2=80=99t feel like they know enough quite =
yet.=E2=80=9D This is, obviously, why the race to define Harris in the next=
 few weeks is so critical for both campaigns. Democratic and Republican vot=
ers view Harris through their expected partisan lenses. And Trump, of cours=
e, is fully defined across the spectrum. But our poll suggests that opinion=
s of Harris are still being formed among independent voters. Their
eventual views might sway the entire election.<br/><br/>We also found in ou=
r poll that Biden made the right decision to leave the race, at least accor=
ding to Democrats. The Echelon poll asked Democrats, =E2=80=9CWhich do you =
think would be the best decision for Biden=E2=80=99s legacy, even if neithe=
r option is perfect?=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94to run for a second term, or =E2=80=
=9Cleaving the race to make room for the next generation of Democratic lead=
ers.=E2=80=9D About half of Democrats (49 percent) said it would be better =
for Biden=E2=80=99s legacy to leave the race. And again, that was before he=
 made his actual announcement. Only 40 percent of Democrats said it would b=
e better for Biden=E2=80=99s legacy to run for a second term.<br/><br/>As f=
or the rush by Democratic elites to anoint Harris as the nominee, most Demo=
cratic voters were supportive, but not exactly a dominant majority. Asked i=
f they wanted Harris or someone else if Biden were to leave the race, 62 pe=
rcent of Democratic voters said Harris, while 18 percent said
someone else, and 20 percent were unsure. <br/><br/>Democratic voters also =
seem more open than party insiders to the idea of a competitive nomination =
before or at the Democratic convention, whatever shape that would take. As =
of Sunday, 48 percent of Democrats said they want a quick vote for Harris t=
o become the nominee, versus 38 percent who said they favored =E2=80=9Can o=
pen contest at the convention with numerous candidates.=E2=80=9D But with D=
emocrats in Washington rallying around Harris as their horse for November, =
it seems clear the latter group will not get their wish.<br/></td>
